#ManifestDigitalCover | The Shefali Shah Effect
For over three decades, Shefali Shah (@shefalishahofficial) has built a career on instinct, honesty, and an unwavering refusal to fit into boxes. From the unforgettable Pyari in Satya to the formidable DCP Vartika Chaturvedi in Delhi Crime, her performances have never relied on noise — only truth.
“A few years ago, if someone told me a woman in her mid-forties would lead a show, I would have laughed. And if they said it would be me, I would have laughed louder,” she says.
In our latest digital cover, the actor reflects on evolving with time, finding freedom in flawed characters, 25 years of marriage, and why success today means something far quieter than fame.

An actor, an author and an agricultural scientist walk into a gala.
On Thursday night in Manhattan, stars like Ethan Hawke and Noah Wylie mingled with Dakota Johnson, Jennie and Hilary Duff at the Time 100 Gala. The annual list highlights the most important people of the year, from stage and screen to activism and politics. This year’s list includes President Trump, Pope Leo XIV and the YouTuber MrBeast. Comedian Nikki Glaser lightly roasted her fellow honorees, inviting the crowd to “drink enough to forget” about their unlikely bedfellows on the list.
Claire Danes sipped martinis with Alan Cumming, Victoria Beckham caught up with Zoe Saldaña and Natasha Lyonne and Anderson .Paak whispered the night away.

posts.caption:
This month, Vogue did something it had never done before, something most people thought it would probably never do: It put two 76-year-old women on its cover. Sure, they weren’t just any 76-year-olds. They were Meryl Streep and the Vogue overseer Anna Wintour, and they were there in a meta way to discuss the mythology around “The Devil Wears Prada 2,” in which Streep plays a version of Wintour. But still, 76.
The most recent round of fashion shows, which our fashion critic Vanessa Friedman attended last month, were notable not only for the almost total lack of size diversity on the runway, but also for the fact that they were, at the same time, taking one giant step forward when it came to another aspect of inclusivity: Age.
The Chanel show opened with 50-year-old Stephanie Cavalli, who was one of 15 models over 40 on its runway. Bottega Veneta had nine older models. Tom Ford, nine (women and men). Givenchy, eight. Balenciaga, five. Louis Vuitton, four. Representation of women of a certain age has never been higher. What’s going on and why now? At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman delves into the striking shift.
posts.alt_text: Photo by New York Times Fashion & Style on April 24, 2026. May be an image of one or more people, makeup, people standing, magazine, turtleneck and text that says 'Why Fashion Suddenly Loves Older Women 魚'.

Planning a wedding can be dizzying. Within minutes of sharing news of an engagement, the questions — and opinions — start rolling in. When is the big day? What kind of celebration will it be? Who’s invited? Will there be cake?
But you can head off a lot of the stress by sitting down with your partner early on and coming up with a clear vision of what you want. Identifying your priorities, desires and budget before diving into the details will simplify the process and keep you on track. To help you navigate these decisions, the experts at The New York Times Wedding section created a quiz to determine what kind of wedding is the best fit for you. Tap the link in our bio to take the quiz. Illustrations by @jessicahische

posts.caption:
At a starry premiere for "The Devil Wears Prada 2," fashion industry figures reflected on just how much the original film got right.
The movie, released in 2006, starred Anne Hathaway as the blunder-prone assistant to Miranda Priestly, a fashion magazine editor played by Meryl Streep who has a gaze withering enough to penetrate the darkest of sunglasses. Now, the sequel considers how their dynamic — and the media landscape they occupy — has shifted after two decades.
At the film’s premiere in Manhattan on Monday night, Hathaway and Streep posed with their co-stars Stanley Tucci and Emily Blunt on a red carpet outside Lincoln Center. Also present were the real-life inspirations, Vogue's Anna Wintour and the head of editorial content for American Vogue, Chloe Malle.
Inside David Geffen Hall, industry figures reflected on how much the movie had in common with the world it portrays. Tap the link in our bio for highlights from the premiere. Visuals by @apisukh

"I’m a psychotherapist with a lot of white-collar clients. I’m pretty heavily tattooed, so wearing a suit helps me seem more credible. However, I am tripping on one rule: Why is it forbidden to button both buttons of a suit jacket?" a reader asked Vanessa Friedman, chief fashion critic for The New York Times. "It gives me better posture and makes me look more together, but am I embarrassing myself if I continue to ignore this?"
When wearing a two-button suit jacket, one is generally advised to leave the bottom button open. At the link in our bio, @vvfriedman explains the reasoning behind the rule. Photo by @apnews

posts.caption:
At Coachella last Friday afternoon, fans rushed to claim a spot in front of one of the festival stages. But the crowd wasn’t camping out for that night’s headliner, Sabrina Carpenter, or for the buzzy girl group Katseye. Attendees were patiently waiting to see Bob Baker Marionette Theater, home to some of Los Angeles’s most popular and beloved puppets.
Started in 1963 by a duo of puppeteers, Bob Baker and Alton Wood, the institution is one of the largest and longest-running puppet theaters in the U.S. But the theater has faced the prospect of closure several times over the past few decades, so performing at Coachella was a major moment for the group.
Mary Fagot, co-executive director of the theater, said that joining the festival lineup felt natural. “We were like, Of course you’re reaching out to us,” Fagot said with a laugh. “Coachella needs us to bring the real and the connected and the joy and the fun and the sense of not taking yourself too seriously.”
Get a closer look at the puppets' Coachella debut and learn more about Bob Baker Marionette Theater at the link in our bio. Visuals by @ye.fan and @dontgabalot

posts.caption:
Could #Madonna’s stolen 2006 #Gucci jacket by #FridaGiannini spark a quiet revival? We all know and love #TomFord’s Gucci, but at this point it’s been overdone. Could Giannini’s era be the next interesting Gucci archive for true fashion girlies to pull from?
Frida had a difficult role stepping in after Ford, yet her version of Gucci brought stability and a different kind of glamour. This was the Gucci I grew up with and she made the brand feel polished and aspirational. A major part of her impact came through accessories, strengthening Gucci’s position as a leader in handbags and leather goods.
She loved jewel tones and metallics, and her references to the 1970s set the groundwork for what would come from #AlessandroMichele. While Michele pushed those references into maximalism and eccentricity, Frida’s work feels more archival than theatrical.
What did you think of Frida’s @gucci ?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on April 24, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'Forget Ford, I Want To See Frida's Gucci Again'.

posts.caption:
They say you can tell when a designer loves the women they’re dressing, and that’s pretty obvious with #SarahBurton’s #Givenchy. Her latest collection moved between tailoring and draping, a reflection of the modern women she is designing for.
One of the most striking looks was the floral dress, an adaptation of a painting by Filipino artist Olan Ventura. Featuring cascading strands of fringe which distorted the floral motifs across the dress so they appeared almost blurred or melting.
The headpieces, by @stephenjonesmillinery , were actually Silk T-shirts twisted and wrapped around the models’ heads, forming sculptural shapes reminiscent of Dutch Golden Age portraiture, specifically Rogier van der Weyden’s Woman with a Winged Bonnet.
What did you think of the collection?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on March 07, 2026. May be an image of magazine, poster and text that says 'Sarah Burton Loves the Women She Designs For'.

posts.caption:
#Dior has been making some excellent red carpet looks recently and I can’t help but notice @jonathan.anderson’s revival of the Victorian bustle.
The bustle emerged in the late 19th century as a padded or structured undergarment worn beneath skirts to create volume at the back of the body, emphasizing a narrow waist. They relied on layers of fabric, pleating, and internal frameworks. Aptly linking #ChristianDior’s 1947 “New Look,” emphasising a cinched waist and structured hips.
#JonathanAnderson’s reinterpretation focuses on sculptural volume, creating shapes through draped panels, bows, or layered fabrics, creating extremely sculptural and romantic silhouettes. We’ve seen this in gowns, skirts, tailoring, and shorts, featuring pleated or structured volumes attached to the back of garments, expanding the concept of the bustle beyond traditional #HauteCouture.
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on March 16, 2026. May be an image of magazine, gown, poster and text that says 'The Bustle Is Back TheBustleIsBackat at Jonathan Anderson's Dior'.

posts.caption:
For his Spring 2025 collection, @markgong_official was inspired by the beloved #CharlotteYork, marking the conclusion of a multi-season exploration of #SexandtheCity characters.
The runway was staged like her Upper East Side apartment, and the clothes reflected Charlotte’s signature aesthetic of polka dots, florals, gingham, and tailoring. The opening and most iconic look was a cream dress called “the Lily dress,” referencing a scene from the second movie in which Charlotte’s vintage #Valentino skirt is ruined by her daughter. #MarkGong reimagined the skirt turning the paint marks into hand beaded handprint details.
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on April 26, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'The Return of Charlotte York's S Ruined Skirt'.

posts.caption:
Seeing #ElsaSchiaparelli’s archive of perfumes at the @vamuseum made me realise…why are (most) luxury perfume bottles so boring now?
#Schiaparelli worked with creative collaborators such as Jean-Michel Frank, #SalvadorDalí, Leonor Fini, and Marcel Vertès to produce bottles and advertisements that were playful, imaginative, and sometimes shocking. Her most famous perfume, Shocking (1937), came in a bottle shaped like a woman’s torso, inspired by #MaeWest. Another example is Snuff, her only men’s perfume, designed in the shape of a pipe (see slide 11)
Today #perfume bottles today feel so minimal, focusing on consistent, recognisable designs. I guess clean, simple bottles are easier to produce, ship, and display, while complex shapes are more expensive to manufacture and more fragile to handle at scale. Culturally, contemporary luxury marketing often prioritises advertising and celebrity endorsements, shifting creativity away from the bottle itself.
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on April 23, 2026. May be an image of fragrance, flask, perfume, bottle and text that says 'fou ou ou Shiaparelli Schicoperell de Seces SomiFou t PARIS Can We Please Bring Back Fun Perfume Bottles?'.

posts.caption:
Remember that viral #Loewe tomato bag? (Shoutout @conndo) Well, it seems #JonathanAnderson’s love for fruit and vegetables has left a lasting impression on Loewe’s designs. Their latest £4,000 leather artichoke bag makes you wonder, why are they so obsessed with fruit and vegetables?
These items aren’t just gimmicks; they’re demonstrations of Loewe’s craftsmanship, requiring intricate pattern cutting, moulding, and a high level of attention to detail. It also pays homage to the brand’s Spanish roots, drawing on a Mediterranean aesthetic of fresh produce and wellness. It aligns with a more relaxed, lifestyle-driven idea of luxury.
There’s also a sense of surrealism, where ordinary objects are elevated. That’s why these pieces feel more like wearable art than conventional accessories. Would you buy your 5 a day from @loewe ?
posts.alt_text: Photo by umar on April 22, 2026. May be an image of fruit, artichoke and text that says 'たバー Why Is Loewe Obsessed With Fruit & Vegetables?'.

posts.caption:
#Opinion | Standup comedian Kaneez Surka @kaneezsurka recently opened up about the "beauty of divorce" on a podcast by Aditi Chandanani @therapywith_aditivc. "Oh you MUST get divorced!" Kaneez quipped, getting Aditi all excited about 'life 2.0'. "When you get divorced, society just drops all expectations of you... they give up on you, and the giving up is the best part!" Kaneez expressed.
Divorce used to be a woman's cue to hide way from society; a label that crept into every introduction. She is just a woman anymore; she is a divorcee. But the women of 2026 are rewriting what it means to walk away from an unfulfilling marriage. They are refusing to carry endings like shame, turning divorce into a cultural statement. Earlier, divorce defined the woman. Now, the woman defines the divorce.
A woman who leaves an unhappy marriage disrupts a comforting myth - that self sacrifice is the highest form of femininity. When she says, “This is not working for me,” she exposes how many others are quietly tolerating what does not work for them either. And that can feel threatening to many.  But here is the truth women know in their bones. Divorce doesn't celebrate heartbreak; it is usually the aftermath of it. So why should women carry the shame of something that often requires immense courage? Why should they be the ones apologising for choosing peace?
Read the full article at www.shethepeople.tv | Views expressed by the author are their own.
#divorce #marriage #culture kim kardashian, all's fair, legal drama shows, revenge dress, divorce party, single women princess diana revenge dress laura wasser

posts.caption:
Throughout history, women have made various efforts to make this world more just and sustainable. However, their initiatives were always treated as of secondary importance and did not receive much public recognition and support as they deserved.
India, a country which is known for its diversity in many aspects, including flora and fauna, has seen many women-led environmental movements. Indian women’s environmental engagement predates the formal label of ecofeminism; traditional roles in water management, agriculture and forest-based livelihoods made women custodians of ecological knowledge.
Ecofeminism in India is not only theoretical, but it is lived, practised, and continually negotiated in movements where gender, ecology, culture and community intersect. This Earth Day, take a look at some major women-led environmental movements.
Visit www.shethepeople.tv to read the full article by Ekta Singh, a PhD Scholar in the Discipline of Gender Studies at Banaras Hindu University. | Views expressed by the author are their own.

Fashion styling has a rate. It always has. The problem is that this industry has never been transparent about what that rate is, and for decades stylists have absorbed the consequences of that silence.
The work starts before the shoot. The expertise takes years to build. The pressure is carried quietly so nobody else has to feel it. And when that work generates commercial value for a brand, a client, or a campaign, it is not a favour. It is a job. And jobs have rates.
We polled over 1,200 stylists across every sector of the industry because we believe that access to this information should not be a privilege. It should be the starting point.
If you are a stylist who has ever quoted blind, undercharged because you were not sure, or struggled to explain your worth to a client, this is for you.
If you are a client, a brand, or someone who has ever wondered what a fashion stylist actually charges and why, this is for you too.

posts.caption:
Fashion styling is a job. Not a hobby. Not a passion project. Not something people do on the side because they love clothes.
It is a profession that takes years to build. The eye. The contacts. The knowledge of how the industry actually works across every sector, every brief, every type of client. That does not come from loving fashion. It comes from years of showing up, doing the work and figuring it out mostly alone.
And yet it is still one of the most undervalued roles in the creative industry.
Stylists are asked to work for free. For credit. For exposure. For a rate that does not come close to reflecting the skill, the hours or the experience involved. And somewhere along the way the industry decided that was normal. That stylists should be grateful for the opportunity. That loving what you do means you should not expect to be paid properly for it.
We are past that.
Behind every iconic image, every red carpet moment, every campaign that stopped you mid-scroll, there was a stylist. Building the looks, interpreting the brief, managing the pressure, absorbing the chaos so nobody else had to. Often without a credit. Sometimes without a proper rate.
That changes when stylists know their worth, charge it without apology and stop accepting the jobs that treat their career like a favour.
➡️➡️ If you are a working fashion stylist who is ready to do exactly that, comment STUDIO and we will send you the free trial link to The Studio of Stylist Elixir. Rate guides. Contracts. Portfolio tools. Masterclasses every Monday. Everything you need to build a career that reflects what you are actually worth.
Save this. Send it to a stylist who needs to hear it.

posts.caption: If something needs such in-depth analysis to be understood than the assignment was a failure! It’s not a dissertation on the politics of 17th century France. It’s CLOTHING! It’s a fashion show and since fashion shows have been able to be seen by millions and consumed in photos and video for decades, those who are not in the loop to see the clothes for real are only left with what we see and to dare to say that just because a design has “References” or inner history negates it from being judged as what it simply LOOKS LIKE, is insultingly arrogant, something that takes 4,000 hours of the most intricate and elaborate technique… CAN STILL LOOK HORRID! All that time does not acquit it of its ugliness or stupidity of design! When all that history and back story sgd references WORK IN CONJUNCTION with the clothes to crest something magical (Blazy/Chanel) then the framework of all that in depth research and application of history is breathtaking, when it’s like JWA/Dior, it just comes across as over focus on ideas that then translate into nothing more than dissertations on a thought than the true purpose of what is happening… MAKING BEAUTIFUL CLOTHES! And for you to suggest that unless you’re erudite and informed of history that you shouldn’t comment is the height of condescension! Anyone can see! And no matter how layered the meaning, if it looks like all that time taken to produce garbage still looks like GARBAGE, it changes not its beauty because you have the knowing of the thought processes and HISTOIRE D’MAISON behind you! It’s pretentious and asinine

posts.caption:
Every fashion stylist has quiet months.
The diary thins out. The enquiries slow. The phone goes quiet. The work that was busy two
weeks ago is suddenly nowhere. And the panic starts.
The panic is the part that does the damage. Not the quiet months. The panic is what makes you
drop the rate, take the wrong job, email a client you would not normally email.
Fashion has seasons. Production has seasons. Editorial has cycles. Brands plan in quarters.
Even the busiest stylists in the industry have weeks where the diary is light.
The quiet is for pitching. For planning. For rest. The work that comes in busy months is the work
that was pitched in quiet months.
Quiet months are not failure. They are part of the rhythm.
Comment HELP to learn how Stylist Elixir can help you build your fashion styling career.
With love, Em x
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on April 26, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'The Quiet Months in Fashion Styling Are Not Failure. Styllst StylistElixir Elixir'.

posts.caption:
There is a misconception about what a fashion stylist actually does.
Most people think it is picking the outfit. Choosing the dress. Showing up on shoot day. That is the visible part. It is also five percent of the job.
The actual job is the work nobody sees. The brief. The research. The references. The pull. The fittings. The alterations. The packing. The travel. The returns. The shipping. The invoices. The follow ups. The kit. The team. The protection of the work itself.
A fashion stylist is a project manager, a researcher, a logistics co-ordinator, a relationship manager, and a creative director, who happens to know what looks good on a body.
The day rate is for all of it.
And then there are the things a stylist should not be doing. Running the career like a hobby. Working for free. Picking outfits over text. Working for credit. Absorbing the costs. None of these are the job. All of them get asked for. All of them get politely turned down.
With love, Em x
posts.alt_text: Photo by Stylist Elixir on April 25, 2026. May be a graphic of magazine and text that says 'What a Fashion Stylist Actually Does. And What They Do Not. Stylist Elixi'.

posts.caption:
There is a scarcity around the rate of a fashion stylist. And there are a number of reasons for it.
We love gatekeeping. We do not want people knowing what we charge because we charge different clients different amounts. The industry is saturated with stylists undercutting. The top dogs who run the industry do not talk, will not share rates, and are generally stuck in their old ways. Their world is not affected, so they do nothing to help.
Fair enough.
But what about everyone else? What about the stylists in the middle? The ones who do not have the top celebrity clients. The ones who do not live in a fashion city. The up and coming ones who do not know what to charge. The ones who have been in it ten years and are still guessing.
That is who this is for❗️
Every stylist is different. Every client is different. Every job is different. Different rates in different sectors of styling. Different rates in different countries. Some countries have active unions helping stylists. Some do not. Some parts of the styling world are rate regulated. Some are not.
So no. This is not telling you what to charge.
This is how to work it out.
The Rate Build. Five parts. Run every time a new enquiry comes in.
Scope. Tier. Load. Extras. Minimum.
The framework is in the carousel. The data that goes with it is inside The Studio of Stylist Elixir, a paid membership community for fashion stylists. Rate guides built from data across every sector. Pitch frameworks. Portfolio teardowns. Sourcing systems. Contract templates. Community calls. Everything you need to price, pitch, protect, and grow a styling career.
Comment RATES for a free trial link.
With love, Em x

posts.caption:
Priyanka Shah (@pri_ism) is a creative director, stylist, and artist based in Mumbai. Trained as a communication designer, her practice has evolved from graphic design into a richly tactile, multidisciplinary language that sits at the intersection of art, design, craft and narrative building. After practising communication design for seven years, Shah grew creatively restless and quit her job to start a self-initiated project involving foraging botanicals to create plant-based sculptures. “It was instinctive and exploratory. Less about outcome, more about process,” she notes.
It was this impulse toward the tactile that culminated in the founding of Studio Lalala, a practice that is rooted in immersive storytelling. The studio’s body of work now spans installations, creating spatial environments, styling, and designing sculptural objects. At its core, the practice is less about the medium and more about the narrative and approach. “At its core, my work is about storytelling through material. Whether we are creating a window display, styling a shoot, designing an installation, or building absculptural object, the intention is the same: to create an experience that evokes emotion and curiosity.”
Growing up, creativity wasn’t just a hobby for her, but a part of her life, with her father taking her to work and showing her screen printing, offset printing, and various other industrial processes. “My parents always encouraged curiosity and experimentation. Looking back, I realise that my practice is simply an extension of that childhood, of being fascinated by materials, processes, and the magic of making.” This same curiosity now
informs everything she does, whether in installations, object-making, or fantastical environments; the core of the practice is informed by research, process, and deep
exploration. “I really, really enjoy diving into new  materials, learning from artisans, and pushing ourselves into unfamiliar territories. Learning as we go is a fundamental part of the studio’s DNA.”
📸: Priyanka Shah (@pri_ism)
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Established on April 24, 2026 tagging @pri_ism. May be an image of poster, microphone and text that says 'ESTD TALENT with Priyanka shah'.

posts.caption:
A momo isn’t just a ₹30 street snack. A Manipuri saree isn’t a ₹3,000 knockoff. Sacred shawls aren’t table runners.
When culture is stripped of context, representation slips into appropriation — and the cost runs deeper than money.
North East India is visible, yet still invisibilised. Newspaper coverage? Just 1.48% of space. OTT shows turn lived realities into “tribal survival” spectacles.
As Binita Chamling puts it: “When visibility itself is inaccurate, identity becomes fragile.” This is what erasure looks like — skill lost, nuance flattened, dignity compromised.
Representation requires context, respect, and care. Anything less reduces culture to shorthand.
It’s time to move beyond exoticising, and start paying attention.
Read more at the link in bio.
By Karishma Kuenzang ( @kkuenzang )
#NorthEastIndia #RepresentationMatters #CulturalCritique #IdentityInIndia #TheEstablished

posts.caption:
Ozempic in India moved from a niche indulgence to something far more everyday in a very short span. Trainers and chemists are part of the distribution, and women are using it at significantly higher rates than men. The benefits are real — improved metabolic health, support for PCOS — but the context has shifted.
What was once used as therapy is increasingly being approached as a shortcut, and the risks are beginning to show. It’s the wellness solution everyone is talking about. The question is what that access is actually building towards.

posts.caption:
Heartbreak doesn’t stay internal for long. It tends to show up in ways that are easy to spot. The haircut. The tattoo. The shift that feels small but rarely is.
What reads as impulse is often something else. A way of taking control when very little feels steady, or putting distance between who someone was and who they are trying to become.
When loss disrupts routine and identity, changing something visible offers a clear, immediate response. It doesn’t resolve what’s underneath, but it does mark that something has already changed.
Read more on the link in bio
By Ria Bhatia ( @riabbhatia )
#griefcoping #bodyimage #wellnessculture #traumaandidentity #postbreakupglowup

posts.caption:
No one orders the same thing at the table.
Groups arrive with mixed preferences, generations, dietary needs and uneven familiarity with cuisines. One menu holds all of it. That’s where Pan Asian restaurants come in. A single menu spanning multiple Asian cuisines.
Chinese cuisine anchors that experience. It has been around the longest and has adapted to Indian tastes, making it easier to introduce Japanese, Korean, Thai or Vietnamese dishes into the same meal.
Even as single-cuisine restaurants grow, Pan Asian continues to work because of how India eats, social, shared and built around variety.
This isn’t new. Head to the link in bio to see why Pan Asian restaurants continue to dominate and what they reveal about eating out in India.
By Karishma Kuenzang ( @kkuenzang )
#PanAsian #DiningTrends #FoodCulture #IndianDining #RestaurantTrends

posts.caption:
ADHD in women often reads as competence: multitasking, perfectionism, emotional labour. Underneath, it’s chronic overwhelm, time blindness, and exhaustion.
Long  framed as a male disorder, ADHD symptoms in women are overlooked, or misread  as stress. The result is years of self-blame before recognition. Masking, hormonal shifts, and the cost of care in India continue to delay diagnosis and limit access to treatment.
The system isn’t missing ADHD in women. It’s been trained not to see it.
Read more at the link in bio
By Ria Bhatia (@riabbhatia)
#ADHD #MentalHealthAwareness #WomensHealth #Neurodiversity #Wellness

posts.caption:
Did you know Luca Guadagnino designed a hotel in Rome? The director behind Call Me by Your Name and Challengers made his hospitality debut with Palazzo Talìa, a 16th-century building transformed into a 26-room boutique hotel through his design firm, Studio Luca Guadagnino.
The concept is very on brand: each space feels like a stage set. A monumental floral carpet by Nigel Peake winds through the entrance, the lobby features Gae Aulenti chairs and a dramatic Murano chandelier, and the bar’s silver-tiled counters reflect original 18th-century frescoes.
Guadagnino’s studio designed the common areas and the Terrace Suite, while the remaining rooms were curated by Mia Home Design Gallery and Laura Feroldi Studio, creating a layered mix of cinematic vision and classic Italian interiors.
A hotel, but make it auteur.
#LucaGuadagnino #Rome #PalazzoTalia #Design #LuxuryTravel

posts.caption:
Shalom Harlow was the 90s “anti-supermodel,” and that’s exactly why she defined the era. Discovered at a The Cure concert in 1990, she brought a completely different energy to the runway: less polished perfection, more instinctive, slightly offbeat cool. Designers like Versace and McQueen literally built moments around her presence.
She made high fashion feel effortless but never boring. Elegant, but with an edge. The kind of model who could carry a show without looking like she was trying to.
By 1995, she took home the industry’s first Model of the Year award, cementing what everyone already knew: she’s that girl.
#ShalomHarlow #90sFashion #SupermodelEra #FashionHistory #TheCitizensPoste

Built around the SS26 Studio collection, the space leans into fluid tailoring, elevated basics, and that very specific quiet luxury energy the brand is clearly doubling down on.
But the real draw is the environment. Vintage furniture sourced from @galeriepatrickseguin and @jacqueslacoste, a curated literary selection from @librairieyvonlambert, coffee from @cafenuances, and a lineup of talks around design and curation.
It’s less about shopping, more about stepping into a fully built-out aesthetic: fashion, interiors, culture, all in one place.

posts.caption: I am hoping young people start a new music festival that counters what Coachella has become, which is nothing about celebrating underground music but merely another “event” for the wealthy and entitled. As someone who went to the first ones, it was inexpensive, accessible, and inclusive. Not anymore. The idea of “festival clothes” is horrible. In my day, we wore the same clothes to a festival that we wore everyday because we were authentic, meaning the fashion was authentic. Fashion needs a new underground explosion because right now it has nothing to say and it’s reflected in the music it dresses.

posts.caption: Warning to anyone wanting to buy with Vestiaire…. Don’t do it. Bought a LV purse but instantly questioned authenticity (literally as soon as I purchased it) went straight on chat about this. They kindly offered to verify it. The purse came to me and it definitely appeared fake to me…. No heat stamp inside, no serial number, very thin cheap dust bag. Hardware not typical of LV. Sent it off for verification. Waited a few days and today I get a notification to say it’s been verified and is authentic…. 🫢 I do not believe it for a second. Go on to chat to discuss, they say it’s got a serial number (seller also told me no serial number as well as when I looked myself) so I asked where the serial number is and could I have a picture… bearing in mind now they have it they are refusing to refund me and they are listing it to be resold. They’ve said they can’t send me a picture and can’t provide me where it is. Which all makes no sense to me whatsoever. I’ve reminded them that me asking for that serial number is me asking for that of my property. I don’t believe it’s real and therefore don’t wish to keep it but it is mine. Asked to speak to a manager and denied. I mean I may get a refund if it sells in 2yrs? What if it doesn’t sell at all? I imagine that I won’t see that listing and really they will do anything with it because they have it in their possession even though it’s mine?? Absolutely vile company. I am a small buyer I’ve not spent thousands. I know me not purchasing will make no difference really but I did want to warn others. I will never use them again, I’m going to make a claim on PayPal and leave a trust pilot review…. It’s a good job I guess that it wasn’t something worth hundreds or thousands. Very very bad company.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee (@bajpayee.manoj) is ridiculously youthful-looking for a 50-something. He’s trim, his face glows, he’s light on his feet. His body of work is already impressive and curated, and his physical self suggests the same level of care and attention. “You sacrifice a lot and don’t compromise with your daily routine,” says Bajpayee. It’s been 17 years since he started skipping dinners, fitness advice that has now become a habit. His work follows the same rigour.
From the beginning, there’s been a palpable hunger in Bajpayee’s progression—an unwavering journey towards excellence that has been present ever since he became the centrepiece of Ram Gopal Varma’s classic crime drama ‘Satya’ as the flamboyant gangster Bhiku Mhatre in 1998. Over the years, the actor became the name to go to for films and shows that demanded an assiduous unpacking of characters with his ability to not just inhabit a part but also make its impact linger beyond the end credits. Today, he’s at the top of his game, occupying a position that once was the late Irrfan’s, straddling mainstream, indie, and middle-of-the-road projects.
Nila Iyer (@nilaiyer) sits down with Bajpayee to discuss films, family, and his 30-year-long career.

posts.caption:
Manoj Bajpayee’s (@bajpayee.manoj) filmography is a “mixed bag”, he points out—by design. His upcoming projects include ‘Governor’ directed by Chinmay Mandlekar, and a reunion with Ram Gopal Varma for the horror comedy ‘Police Station Mein Bhoot’. Neeraj Pandey’s crime thriller for Netflix is also in the pipeline, as is American director Ben Rekhi’s upcoming adaptation of Arvind Adiga’s novel ‘Last Man in Tower’.
Right now, Bajpayee is at that sweet spot in his career where he can take the long view. On screen, he has played losers and heroes, but IRL he’s able to balance celebrity with craft and Bollywood’s ruthless demands with his individual ambitions. For him, mainstream cinema is a strategic choice. “The Hindi film industry is very market-oriented, so to keep one’s value alive one has to balance it with things on which I don’t compromise,” Bajpayee explains.
Read Nila Iyer’s (@nilaiyer) full interview with the Bollywood actor at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Last year, designer and Madraswear founder Purushu Arie accused Chennai-based designer Vivek Karunakaran of copying his work—his innovations with tailored lungis, 𝐯𝐞𝐭𝐭𝐢𝐬 and a distinctly Tamil design language. Karunakaran maintained that his work was independently developed.
At 𝑻𝑽𝑶𝑭, we invited both to present their views—on design and copyright. The counterpoint, however, never ran. Because Arie’s argument moved beyond design authorship into caste, class, and privilege. We did not want not adjudicate the caste versus privilege terrain within a design plagiarism debate. I saw them as relevant but separate issues. Arie withdrew his article. We respected that decision.
Yet, part of what he wrote stayed with me. “My design authorship cannot be detached from its politics of assertion and resistance. Without that context, it is reduced to surface ornamentation—like temple colour schemes on Madras checks.”
Temple colours on Madras checks was a stinging metaphor. That what is worn on the street, shaped by stigma and social geography, is acceptable only after validation. Arie said he had been mocked for his work until validated by the press. Innovation from the margins he believes is often dismissed.
The argument knocks on a door that the fashion industry rarely opens.
April is Dalit History Month.
Yet, not much on caste has surfaced in Indian fashion media—not this month, not across the year. Indian fashion speaks fluently about craft. It debates cultural appropriation, now increasingly so, given the growing disregard to Indian-origin work and skills by Western brands. Textile-craft exhibitions foreground artisan visibility. But when it comes to caste—within which much of the labour exists—the conversation falls silent.
As Suraj Yengde writes in 𝑪𝒂𝒔𝒕𝒆 𝑴𝒂𝒕𝒕𝒆𝒓𝒔, caste is not a system waiting to disappear; it is graded hierarchy that mutates, and survives within modern institutions.
Fashion is no exception. It wears hierarchy on its sleeve. The lead image from Chamar Studio applauds its founder Sudheer Rajbhar for turning a slur into a brand. Read the rest on the link in bio.
#dalithistorymonth #casteinfashion #politicsofdesign
🖋️ @shefatwork
posts.alt_text: Photo shared by The Voice Of Fashion on April 24, 2026 tagging @purushuarie, @shefatwork, @chamarstudio, @madraswear, and @suraj.yengde. posts.caption:
From the Ralph Lauren bandhani-print skirt and 𝒋𝒉𝒖𝒎𝒌𝒂 earrings, to Dior’s recent take on the 𝒃𝒂𝒏𝒅𝒉𝒈𝒂𝒍𝒂 and Prada’s Kolhapuri-inspired leather footwear—global fashion’s fascination with repackaging Indian crafts and textiles and giving them a six-figure price tag is peaking in the debates on cultural appropriation. As Ritu Sethi, founder-trustee of the Craft Revival Trust, points out: “Colonised nations have long been emporiums of patterns, designs and products that those in power have been picking and choosing from for many centuries. That is why the 𝒂𝒎𝒃𝒊 became the paisley, and you will see the ubiquitous ‘Indian prints’ or ‘African design’ in books.”
But in a market where motifs, techniques and narratives are copied and mass-produced—both by the domestic unorganised sector and international players— Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) is what can separate authenticity from cultural extraction. Safir Anand, senior partner and head (Trademarks, Contractual and Commercial IP) at Delhi-based law firm Anand & Anand believes that the protection of Indian legacy often fails not because tools are unavailable, but because they are misunderstood.
This article, that first appeared in 𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝑽𝒐𝒊𝒄𝒆 𝒐𝒇 𝑭𝒂𝒔𝒉𝒊𝒐𝒏'𝒔 luxury white paper 𝑻𝒉𝒆 𝑨𝒕𝒍𝒂𝒔 𝒐𝒇 𝑨𝒇𝒇𝒍𝒖𝒆𝒏𝒄𝒆 2026 on The Rise of Indian Luxury, examines how copyrights can safeguard creative expression and why filing for protection and taking legal recourse is no longer optional for Indian brands. Link in bio.
🖋️ @kimidangor
#thevofashion #theatlasofaffluence2026
#IPR

posts.caption:
Hi, I’m Veronica 👋🏼 a sewing pattern designer based in Melbourne, Australia.
I create patterns, tools and resources that help you sew with understanding.
My work focuses on how fabrics behave, how garments are constructed, and how sewing connects us to the history of what we wear.
Sewing Patterns
My patterns are designed for versatility, wearability and fun. They include the Hazel, Freya, Odette, Audrey, Hera, Cordelia and the Sleeve Studio — each combining comfort with thoughtful design details. You’ll also find The Noelle, a free beginner-friendly pattern that introduces my approach to fit and construction.
The Paper Doll
A print-at-home paper doll designed to help you visualise and play with my sewing patterns. You can cut it out and colour it yourself, or use the pre-filled versions featuring my fabric prints and colour palettes. The set includes curvier model options and three skin tones to choose from.
The Conscious Consumer eBook
A practical guide to fabrics, sustainability and sewing with intention. It helps you understand fibre origins, weave structures and how to make long-lasting choices when you sew or buy clothes.
Digital Sewing Tools
Two paid calculators designed for precision:
• Shirring Calculator – works out fabric shrinkage and elastic thread length.
• GSM Calculator – estimates fabric weight and drape.
Available individually or as a discounted bundle.

I love fibres and fabrics and if you do too, this is where it all started…6000 years ago.
This is the first piece in my new series The Goddess Project, follow along.

posts.caption:
I rewatched Anastasia and then spent three hours finding out everything I didn’t know about it as a kid. Fair warning: this film is not historically accurate, and by the end of these slides you’ll understand exactly why that matters.
Also how did we KNOW the blue dress was velvet without knowing it was velvet?
But first, Meg Ryan had to be convinced to take the role, Liz Callaway sang every song you love, and Jim Cummings voiced two of the most iconic villain songs of the 90s without getting nearly enough credit for either.
The further I went, the more complicated it got. The film is built on a rumour that was already disproven before it came out. It gave the revolution a face so the Romanovs didn’t have to have one. And Anastasia was canonised as a saint in 2000, a detail Western nostalgia tends to skip past entirely.
I still love this film. I’m just sitting with it differently now. What’s your favourite part? 🙊
posts.alt_text: Photo by Veronica Tucker the Label on April 22, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'I rewatched Anastasia for the first time since I was a kid. Then I did the research. veronica tucker'.

posts.caption:
What happens when you put Meryl Streep and Anna Wintour—the two Mirandas, as it were—in a room together? A wide-ranging conversation about fashion, family, friendship, and yes, the forthcoming sequel to “The Devil Wears Prada.”
“In terms of Miranda, and coming back to that character 20 years later, I did think honestly about Anna, and tried to imagine what it was like to carry her responsibility and to be as interested in the world and curious as she must have to be,” Streep tells Greta Gerwig in Vogue’s May 2026 cover story. And what did Wintour do when she heard the rumours that “The Devil Wears Prada 2” was happening? She called Streep, of course. “I knew she would tell me if it was going to be all right,” Wintour says. “I trusted her implicitly.”
Read the full story at the link in bio.

posts.caption:
Siddharth Morakhia (@sanctionedmomo) and Rebecka Morakhia (@rebeckamorakhia) knew they wanted “the chaotic fun of an Indian wedding”, but also a setting with enough history and beauty to stand on its own. Their wedding clothes drew most directly from the family archive. Siddharth wore an heirloom 19th-century choga from an Indian royal house, embroidered in real gold zari and antique Kashmiri tilla, with a sage green silk organza cape appliquéd with antique sun and moon motifs. Rebecka’s bridal lehenga, made with Gota Patti House, was worked in marodi gold thread and real zari gota so fine, it took more than a year to make. Head to the link in bio to see their wedding up-close.

posts.caption:
Legendary Indian photographer Raghu Rai (@raghurai.official) has passed away at the age of 83. And while he travelled all over India and the world with his camera, he would gather his family at least once a month under the cassia and neem trees of his farmhouse in Delhi, breaking bread and sharing stories, shoes cast off along with outside cares. Early memories of riding in a tractor around the plot of land, “even in the burning summer heat”, are a flash of nostalgia for his daughter Avani, moments of her and her father together. Today, Raghu Rai becomes one with the natural elements he so loved.
Words by: Cosmo Brockway (@cosmobrockway)
Image credits: Avani Rai (@avani.rai)
#VogueCulture

posts.caption: At Dries Van Noten’s Fondazione debut presentation, opening today in Venice, an onslaught of intoxicating meraviglie awaits. Within the lavish embrace of Palazzo Pisani Moretta, whose stuccoed ceilings and ornate rooms are a manifesto of 18th century decorative excess, beauty isn’t simply in residence, it proliferates. Fashion, craft, and art converse fluently, each amplifying the other in a prismatic Wunderkammer, shaped by Van Noten’s almost alchemical curatorial instinct. The designer sat down with @tiziana_cardini to discuss the historic palazzo that houses the presentation, why fashion is merely a punctuation mark in this expansive show, and, naturally, the power of beauty.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 25, 2026. May be an image of text that says 'VOGUE RUNWAY RUN INSIDE THE INAUGURAL PRESENTATION AT DRIES VAN NOTEN'S NEW FONDAZIONE IN VENICE'.

posts.caption: This week is the fifth anniversary of Alber Elbaz’s passing. The designer brought a certain joie de vivre, wit, intuition, empathy, and spontaneous flair to fashion. The kind of thinking he applied to how women are living. The discussion behind it. How he just wanted to make complicated things easy and delightful. Half a decade is a long time, but the relevance of his approach is still on point for now. His clothes are still alive in closets, being worn as favorite fail-safes for evening. They’re in museums and archives, and being purloined by daughters and granddaughters. At the link in bio, @SarahMower_ remembers Alber Elbaz, the beloved designer of Lanvin and AZ Factory, five years after his death.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 25, 2026. May be an image of bowtie, dinner jacket and text.

posts.caption: There are fashion obsessives, and then there’s @jeandenisfranoux. A longtime fashion professor at Studio Berçot and a designer himself from 1994 to 2001, Franoux began collecting perfume bottles as a child before getting hooked on clothes. Today, at 56, Franoux is sitting on an archive of 25,000 pieces and counting and from tonight through Saturday, an invitation-only event at a private space in the Upper Marais will offer a first look at the collective achievement, titled Regarderobes (the name is a portmanteau of the word “look” and “wardrobes”). Days before the opening, he welcomed Vogue to his Paris apartment/storeroom/makeshift photo studio, where hundreds of finds line racks while others, piled in boxes from floor to ceiling, await their close-up. Read more about what to expect at the link in bio.
posts.alt_text: Photo by Vogue Runway on April 23, 2026. May be an image of overcoat, display case, mannequin and text.

@nathanhartono's creative appetite knows no bounds. "I have an insatiable curiosity towards all these different sides, and am still learning more and more every day," our #VogueMan cover star quips.
For our #VogueMan 'Pursuit' issue, the singer-songwriter and actor is captured in numerous unexpected spots dotted around Singapore—each frame teeming with the refreshingly zany mood that seems to colour Hartono's life.
In the link in bio, Hartono delves into his creative worlds, new projects he is working on, and how community sits at the centre of the Singapore arts scene. Preorder a copy of our #VogueMan 'Pursuit' issue, out on newsstands on 15 April.

posts.caption:
There is no looking away. Not from the magnetic force that is @beccca. Whilst it was her breakout role in 'Gap: The Series' that brought her global fame, her staying impact feels relentless. And it's one that starts to make sense when studying her serious credentials across acting, singing, and modelling.
"Each discipline fulfils a different creative side of me. Acting allows me to tell stories, music feels very personal, and modelling connects me with fashion and visual expression," shares #RebeccaArmstrong, who joins us on our April 'Retrofuture' issue as one of two cover stars.
In the link in bio, we delve into the new creative challenges she's taking on in 2026, and what she's been learning about herself along the way.

posts.caption:
@caileespaeny is in between worlds. Having played characters that span across time and space, the Golden Globe-nominated actress has a fascinating plurality about her that feels both familiar and fresh.
Cast in a nostalgic mood that calls to mind the filmography of #DavidLynch and #StanleyKubrick, the actress transforms into a defiant, enigmatic persona as our 'Retrofuture' cover star, lensed by the legendary Canadian photographer @normanjeanroy. "For the shoot, we were tapping into a bit of a David Lynch world. It was so fun to lock into this set and character with the team on this shoot," Spaeny muses.
As one of two cover stars for April's 'Retrofuture' issue, Spaeny lets us into her universe—from her determined portrayal of Rain Carradine in 'Alien: Romulus' to the tender portrait she paints in 'Priscilla' and her newest work for @netflix's limited series 'Beef'. The full cover story, now in the link in bio.

posts.caption:
The Fashion Worldbuilder. The Screen Queen. The Fintech Pioneer. Introducing: the remarkable women in of #VogueLeaders 2026.
“I’ve never seen fashion as just a product, but as a way to shape emotion, perspective and connection. I hope what I create can invite beauty into people’s lives while inspiring a deeper sense of self,” says @bess___ye, the creative force behind Singaporean fashion label @r__y__e. The local brand founder is joined by prolific actress and host @jeanetteaw as well as the Co-founder and CEO of @youtripsg Caecilia Chu, as part of the 2026 class of Vogue Leaders, an annual initiative that shines a spotlight on 10 women who are pushing the envelope and leading the charge for community and change.
Dressed in pieces from @maxmara, these women are seen in a study of presence—where dress moves beyond adornment to sit alongside identity, intention and becoming. Meet the 10 extraordinary women who are part of the 2026 class of Vogue Leaders, and discover their journeys via the link in bio—each offering a distinct perspective on what it means to be remarkable today. #VogueSingapore

posts.caption:
𝙏𝙝𝙚 𝙨𝙝𝙞𝙠𝙖𝙧𝙜𝙖𝙝 𝙎𝙚𝙧𝙞𝙚𝙨 by @divaniindia
In the word “Shikargah” , “Shikar” means hunting and “Gah” means place.
The Shikargah by Divani is representative of a limited edition couture series reinterpreted with finest “ zardozi | ज़रदोज़ी | زردوزی " and the old school techniques of “tukdi ” patching using hand-dyed and handwoven tissue silk. From depicting the typical lion and deer hunt to british influences in designs can be notably seen as a part of the hunting scenes.
Featured here is our limited edition “Shikargah” saree, adorned with handcrafted animal and floral motifs on pure muga silk. The look is completed with an ornate jacket from the same series, featuring rich flora and fauna motifs rendered as an all-over pattern with archival techniques. The ensemble is further accentuated with a Shikargah "topi", highlighted by a striking “Bagh” motif at its centre.
Creative Direction & Styling: @who_wore_what_when

posts.caption:
Rooted in the revival of royal costumes and heirloom traditions, this Farshi Garara traces back to the courts of Awadh and Hyderabad, known for its floor-length, sweeping silhouette.
Featured here is Divani’s classic Farshi Garara in a deep crimson tones, detailed with "lucknawi tukdi ka kaam" and crafted with pure copper wire zardozi craftsmanship. It is paired with a signature kurti, adorned with handcrafted peacock detailings. For the purposes of styling, the ensemble is completed with a "Hyderabadi khada dupatta", an heirloom drape from the royal wardrobes.
Crafted in pure silk tissue, this piece reflects the revival of handloom textiles through our Benarasi looms, bringing back the essence of royal dressings.

posts.caption:
The Great Gatsby. Promising Young Woman. Maestro. There’s no shortage of career highlights for Carey Mulligan. Our April cover star has never shied away from portraying complicated women with rich inner lives, and now, she’s stepping into her messiest role yet. “I was like, ‘Oh, that’s insane and feral. And I loved it,’” Mulligan recalls of reading one particularly unhinged scene in season 2 of Beef, the Emmy-winning dark comedy-drama miniseries in which her character’s marriage devolves into resentment, blackmail, and regret. With the sophomore season hitting Netflix this month and Greta Gerwig’s highly anticipated Narnia adaptation coming later this year, the actress has her hands full with one buzzy project after another. From Mulligan’s real marriage advice to her dream projects, go deeper into our April cover story at the link in our bio.

posts.caption:
It may still be spring, but we're already dreaming of summer. With a new season on the horizon, vacation wardrobes are top of mind, and the 2026 swim landscape feels especially noteworthy.
This season, swimwear is moving in two distinct directions. On one hand, there’s a clear return to refined simplicity—think sleek one-pieces, barely there bikinis, and elegant silhouettes that feel pulled from the '90s but updated with modern precision. On the other, designers are embracing personality through tactile details, nostalgic prints, and unexpected embellishments that feel almost like jewelry for the beach.
Ready to dive in? Head to the link in our bio for the key swim trends defining summer 2026 to know now.

posts.caption:
The #metgala 2016 explored the relationship between fashion and technology, setting a futuristic tone.
The theme, “Manus x Machina,” highlighted the blend of handcraft and machine-made design in couture.
Celebrities embraced metallics, structured silhouettes, and innovative fabrics on the red carpet.
Standout looks included Claire Danes’ glowing Zac Posen gown and Zendaya’s sleek Michael Kors ensemble.
The night proved how fashion can evolve with technology while still honoring craftsmanship.

posts.caption:
For Spring 2025, #MarkGong draws inspiration from the ever-iconic #CharlotteYork, bringing his multi-season tribute to #SexandtheCity characters to a close.
The runway mirrored her Upper East Side apartment, setting the tone for a collection rooted in her polished, romantic style—think polka dots, florals, gingham, and sharp tailoring.
The standout opening look, a cream piece dubbed “the Lily dress,” nods to a memorable moment from the second film.
Referencing the scene where Charlotte’s vintage #Valentino skirt is ruined by her daughter, Mark transforms the mishap into art, recreating the paint stains as delicate hand-beaded handprints.
#womenculture
posts.alt_text: Photo by WOMENCULTURE on April 26, 2026. May be an image of dress and text that says 'FASHION TRENDS The Return of Charlotte York's Ruined Skirt. WOMEN CULTURE'.

posts.caption:
I know “chic” gets thrown around a lot, but this dress genuinely defines it. #carolynbessettekennedy 1996 wedding gown a bias-cut silk slip with a soft cowl neckline stood in sharp contrast to the era’s oversized, heavily embellished bridal styles, instantly reshaping modern bridal fashion.
Created by her close friend Narciso Rodriguez, whom she met during her time at Calvin Klein, the design drew global attention and propelled his career, highlighting his refined tailoring and quiet sensuality.
Though documentation is scarce, Love Story costume designer Rudy Mance painstakingly recreated the gown, sourcing original fabrics and collaborating with the same makers behind the veil and gloves. Through multiple fittings, the dress was perfected to capture the precise bias cut and fluid elegance that made the original unforgettable.
posts.alt_text: Photo by WOMENCULTURE on April 24, 2026. May be an image of newspaper, magazine, dress, wedding and text that says 'WEDDING Was This The Chicest Wedding Dress Ever Made?. WOMen CULTURE'.

posts.caption:
Inside #louisvuitton latest spectacle in London, a grand Mayfair townhouse has been transformed into a lavish, multi-floor “hotel” experience celebrating 130 years of its iconic monogram.
Though not an actual place to stay, the pop-up blends fashion, hospitality, and exhibition immersing visitors in themed rooms inspired by signature bags like the Speedy, Keepall, and Neverfull.
Guests “check in” through the Keepall Lobby before exploring spaces like Café Alma for Parisian-style dining and Bar Noé, an intimate champagne lounge.
Each floor acts as a storytelling canvas, turning luxury travel heritage into a theatrical, Instagram-ready experience rather than a traditional retail store.
Open for a limited time in Berkeley Square, it’s less about accommodation and more about positioning Louis Vuitton as a lifestyle universe where fashion, culture, and fantasy collide.
#louisvuitton #lv #womenculture

posts.caption:
Meet #NazzalStudio a rising voice where fashion becomes a form of resistance and storytelling.
The latest collection blends traditional Palestinian and Bedouin elements with raw, sculptural modern silhouettes.
Each piece carries deeper meaning exploring identity, displacement, and cultural memory through design.
More than clothing, it’s a powerful statement—turning heritage into something visible, wearable, and impossible to ignore.
#runway #foryou #womenculture
📸 : Nazzal studio

posts.caption:
The new @miaou x @adidas collection taps straight into early 2000s WAG energy glam, sporty, and unapologetically feminine.
Inspired by sideline style and icons like Victoria Beckham, it blends football culture with sleek, off-duty luxury.
Corset details, nylon track pants, and metallic sneakers create a mix of softness and edge.
#adidas #sporty #womenculture

posts.caption:
After 283 Grand Prix races, six wins, 39 podiums and three pole positions, Sergio “Checo” Pérez doesn’t have anything left to prove when it comes to his @F1 racing career — but everything left to build.⁠
⁠
Now leading the charge at the Cadillac Formula 1 Team, the Mexican superstar is turning a fresh start into a final statement. ⁠
⁠
"I had unfinished business," he said in WWD's April Weekend cover story. ⁠
Back on the grid and in @TommyHilfiger, Sergio Pérez steps into a new era. ⁠
⁠
Read the interview at the link in bio. ⁠
⁠

posts.caption:
Former football player-turned-fashion-photographer @DeMarcusAllen has opened a wellness space that brings together sculpture, sound, scent, and sport.⁠
⁠
@DreamSevenParis is just steps from Paris’s grand hotels in the 16th arrondissement, including The Peninsula, Plaza Athénée and Shangri-La, and Allen is determined to add another luxury address to the neighborhood with his 4,700-square-foot space.⁠
⁠
Read more about the studio inside WWD Weekend at the link in bio. ⁠
